title: Pensions, savings, and the real cost of a UK career change
dek: Career change advice rarely covers the money side. The real cost is not the tuition. It is the lost-earnings month and the pension you forgot to consolidate. Here's how the maths actually breaks down.
author: switchable_team
cover_image_alt: Spreadsheet showing personal budget breakdown with calculator and pen
excerpt: UK guide to the real money cost of changing career. Lost-earnings month, pension consolidation, the 12-month plan, and the free tools worth using.
body:
## The question nobody asks

Most career change articles open with the same line. "Save six months of income first."

Nobody explains where you get six months of income from. Nobody breaks down what the actual costs are. Nobody helps you do the maths. So the advice sounds responsible without ever being useful.

This is the breakdown that should come first. It will not match every situation. It does explain why so many career changes stall at the money question.

## What a career change actually costs

The cost is almost never the course. Funded training is free. Self-funded short courses are usually under £500. Loan-funded Level 4 to 6 qualifications run higher but the loan defers payment until you earn over £25,000.

The real cost is in three places.

**Lost earnings during transition.** If you switch sectors and start a level lower, you might earn less for six to twelve months. That gap is the biggest line in any honest career change budget. It is also the most predictable. You can model it on a single page.

**Pension contribution gap.** If you leave one employer and join another, the new pension scheme may have a waiting period (often three months) before contributions start. That gap rarely shows up in articles. Over a decade it costs more than most people think.

**Hidden costs.** Travel to a new workplace. Childcare changes because the hours shifted. The wardrobe refresh you swore you would not do but always do. Allow £1,000 to £2,000 for the first six months.

Add those three and the picture is honest. For most adults changing career inside the UK funded training system, the all-in cost is under £5,000 over the first year. Not £30,000. Not "six months of income". The number is usually small enough to plan for.

## The pension question most people miss

By their forties, most UK adults have three to five old pension pots scattered across previous employers. The smallest are often forgotten and quietly losing value to fees.

Before you change jobs is a good time to consolidate them. You can use the government's free Pension Tracing Service to find old pensions you have lost track of, then move them into a single pot with one provider. Providers like Vanguard, PensionBee, and AJ Bell all offer SIPPs (self-invested personal pensions) that take consolidated pots.

Two reasons this matters around a career change.

One: a single consolidated pot is easier to keep contributing to during a transition. If you go self-employed, you can keep paying in. If you take a lower-paid role, you can match the lower contribution rate without losing the old pot's growth.

Two: tax relief on personal contributions still works at 20% basic rate and 40% higher rate. If you are about to drop a tax band, putting some of the higher-earning year's bonus into the pension before the change can be worth four figures in tax relief.

This is not financial advice. It is general information. If your pots add up to more than around £30,000 or you are within ten years of retirement, talk to an independent financial adviser before consolidating. MoneyHelper offers a free service that explains your options and refers you to vetted advisers if you want one.

## The 12-month money plan

Months -3 to 0 (still in current job):

Open a separate savings account labelled "career change". Move a fixed amount each payday. Even £200 a month puts £600 in the buffer before you start.

Find and consolidate any small pension pots under £10,000 sitting with old employers. Use the Pension Tracing Service to find ones you have forgotten.

Map the lost-earnings month. If you switch and earn 20% less for six months, what is the total gap? That is your target buffer.

Months 1 to 3 (transition):

Live on the lower side of the income range you mapped. Keep the savings buffer untouched if at all possible. The buffer is for genuine shortfalls, not lifestyle continuity.

Pause the pension contribution rate to the new employer minimum for three months only. Restore it at month four.

Months 4 to 12 (settling):

Restore pension contributions to whatever level you held before.

Top up the career-change buffer back to its starting balance. You used some of it. That is fine. Refilling it is what makes the next change easier.

Run the maths once at month six. If the new role is paying what you expected and the lost-earnings gap was inside the budget, the plan worked. Move on. If not, look honestly at whether the new role is structurally worth less than the old one or whether you can negotiate a step up at the year mark.

## Free tools worth using

**MoneyHelper.** The government's free money guidance service. Pension consolidation, budgeting, debt, scams. Worth using before any paid adviser.

**Pension Tracing Service.** Free, run by the Department for Work and Pensions. Finds old pension pots from previous employers.

**PensionBee, Vanguard, AJ Bell.** Three of the cleaner UK SIPP providers if you want to consolidate. Compare their fees, not their marketing.

**HMRC Personal Tax Account.** Shows your National Insurance record and current tax position. Useful before you make pension or self-employment decisions.

## When the maths breaks

Pause the career change if any of these are true.

Your "career change" budget is on a credit card you cannot pay off. Debt with interest is the wrong tool for retraining. Pay it down first, even if it means delaying the change by six months.

Your household relies on your current income to a degree that one missed month would force a house move. Build a deeper buffer first. The training will still be there.

You are within five years of retiring. The maths flips. At that point, holding pension contributions steady is more valuable than chasing a new role unless the new role is materially better paid.

The maths usually works. It tends to look worse on paper than it does in practice, because most career changes pay back faster than the article-writers assume. But the maths still has to be honest before you start.

{{related-posts}}

title: How to change career in the UK without quitting first
dek: You don't need a six-month savings buffer or a leap of faith. You need a plan and a few facts most people don't know.
author: switchable_team
cover_image_alt: Adult learner studying at a laptop in a sunlit kitchen
excerpt: Three UK funding routes, five eligibility rules that catch people out, and a four-step start that doesn't need six months of savings or a leap of faith.
body:
## The advice that keeps people stuck

Most career change articles in the UK open the same way. Save six months of income. Quit. Reinvent yourself.

The advice sounds responsible. It also keeps people stuck for years. Few adults in their thirties, forties or fifties have six months of savings sitting around. Fewer are willing to bet the savings on a future they can't yet see. So they stay put. The years go past.

The UK funded training system was built for the opposite case. Adults who already have a job, a mortgage, and an obligation to bring money in next month. The schemes exist. The rules just sit behind a wall of guidance that few people read end to end.

This is the version that matters.

## The three funding routes worth knowing in England

Three schemes pay for most adult retraining in England. Each pays the provider directly. None of them puts the bill on you.

**Free Courses for Jobs (FCFJ).** Pays for a first full Level 3 qualification, the equivalent of A-Levels. Eligible adults are 24 and over, UK-resident, without a Level 3 already, in receipt of a low wage or out of work. Courses cover business, accountancy, counselling, digital, social care, construction and more. Length varies from four days intensive to several months part-time. The course list is set centrally and updates each year.

**Skills Bootcamps.** Twelve to sixteen week intensives, funded by the Department for Education, delivered by approved providers. Eligible adults are 19 and over, UK-resident, with the right to work. The format is built around people who work full time. Most run evenings and weekends. Each cohort includes a guaranteed interview with a hiring partner at the end. Tech-heavy: software, data, cyber, digital marketing, construction, green skills, EV maintenance.

**Advanced Learner Loans.** Government-backed loans for Level 3 to Level 6 qualifications. The loan is paid straight to the provider. You repay nothing until you earn over £25,000. No credit check. Eligible adults are 19 and over, UK-resident. Useful if you already have a Level 3 (so FCFJ doesn't apply) but want to add a higher qualification.

That covers most adult retraining in England. There's also Apprenticeships (any age over 16, levy-funded, no upper limit) and various local pots that open eligibility wider in their regions, often filling the 19-23 age gap that FCFJ leaves open.

## Five eligibility rules that catch people out

**1. The 19 vs 24 age confusion.** Skills Bootcamps start at 19. FCFJ kicks in at 24. Local funding pots often open the 19-23 gap inside their regions. The provider's website usually shows whichever route is most permissive for your postcode, which is why the same course can look fully funded for you and not for a friend three cities away.

**2. The "I already have a Level 3" rule.** FCFJ funds a first full Level 3 only. But an A-Level from 1998 in French Literature does not automatically disqualify you from a 2026 Level 3 in business. Some providers apply a "first Level 3 in this sector" reading. Worth asking before assuming you're out.

**3. Self-employed with no payslips.** The low-wage criteria for FCFJ are commonly read as requiring payslip evidence. Self-employed earnings under the threshold qualify on a Self Assessment SA302 or an HMRC tax-year overview. PAYE not required.

**4. Settled vs pre-settled status.** Both qualify for all three schemes. So does ILR, citizenship, and the right-to-study under most work visas. The eligibility wall is residency, not nationality.

**5. The funding category of your old qualification.** If your Level 3 was vocational (NVQ Level 3, BTEC Level 3) the rules differ slightly from academic Level 3s (A-Levels). Mostly the difference is just wording. Occasionally it opens a route you'd assumed was closed.

If you've read all five and you're still not sure whether you qualify, you're in the same place 81% of UK adults are. That gap is the reason this company exists.

## How to actually start without quitting

The route map is shorter than the advice books make it look.

**This week.** Run your postcode through the government's Find a Course service, or through Switchable's course finder. Both surface real intakes you're eligible for, with the funding route attached. Write down three options that match your sector and your schedule.

**Next two weeks.** Email two providers from the shortlist. Ask one specific question each. "Do you have evening or weekend places on the next intake?" or "Can I defer the start by one cohort?". You're testing how they respond, not committing.

**Within the month.** Pick one course. Enrol on the next intake. The most common mistake is researching everything and starting nothing.

Every step there is doable around the day job. None of them costs you money. The training itself, when it starts, costs you time, not income.

## What we do at Switchable

We help adults across England find funded training that fits the life you already have. The course finder asks a few honest questions about your age, postcode, employment status, and the kind of role you're heading towards. It matches you to providers with current places. No long form or login, takes about two minutes.

If you want to stop reading career-change articles and look at three real options, that's the door.

{{related-posts}}

title: Starting a business in your 40s: a UK route that actually works
dek: Forget the twenty-something founder story. UK adults in their forties start businesses with better odds than anyone else. Here's how to begin without quitting your job.
author: switchable_team
cover_image_alt: Adult founder at a small home workshop, laptop open beside hand tools
excerpt: Five real UK schemes that pay for business training, the four credentials banks and commercial clients actually look for, and the first-year mechanics nobody writes about.
body:
## The founder advice that doesn't fit UK reality

The default advice about starting a business assumes a Silicon Valley setup. Twenty-something founders. Investor money. App that scales. None of which applies if you're 42, in Birmingham, and thinking about doing this around a full-time job.

UK data tells a different story. The Office for National Statistics business births release puts owner-managers aged 40-54 as the largest founder cohort by a clear margin. The average age of a UK founder of a high-growth business is 47. The forties are the strongest founder decade you have, not because of grit, but because of accumulated network, sector knowledge, and money sense.

What's missing is a route map that respects you already have obligations. The UK has one. Most people don't know it exists.

## Five real UK schemes that pay for business training

**1. Free Courses for Jobs (FCFJ).** Funds a first full Level 3 qualification. The SFEDI Level 3 Award in Innovation, Growth and Development and the Level 3 Diploma in Business Administration both sit on the FCFJ list. Eligibility: 24+, UK-resident, no existing Level 3. Format: anything from four-day intensives to several months part-time depending on the provider.

**2. Skills Bootcamps.** Twelve to sixteen week intensives for adults 19+. Most sectors tagged "Skills Bootcamps for Entrepreneurs" or "Digital for Business" qualify. Some are explicitly for people testing a business idea while still employed. Schedule designed around evenings and weekends.

**3. Advanced Learner Loans.** Government-backed loans for Level 4-6 qualifications including business management diplomas. You repay nothing until you earn over £25,000. Useful if you already hold a Level 3 (so FCFJ does not apply) and want the next-step credential a bank or larger client will recognise.

**4. The New Enterprise Allowance was discontinued in late 2022.** This often comes up in older articles. Don't waste time looking for it. Universal Credit claimants now get equivalent support through the standard Jobcentre Plus self-employment route, which includes mentoring through the Enterprise Mentor scheme.

**5. Regional growth hubs.** Most LEPs (Local Enterprise Partnerships, restructured into combined authorities since 2024) still run free or low-cost peer-led business support. Search "growth hub [your region]" on gov.uk. Northern Powerhouse, West Midlands Growth Company and similar bodies all run cohort programmes for early-stage founders that don't appear in the standard course directories.

## The four credentials banks and commercial clients look for

Founder qualifications are a soft signal, but they matter at specific decision points.

**A regulated Level 3 in business** carries weight with lenders applying the FCA's "responsible lending" assessment, particularly for sole traders without two years of trading accounts. Without it, expect to put up personal collateral. With it, terms improve measurably.

**Trade body memberships** in your sector (CIM for marketing, ICAEW for accounting, RICS for property, FCA-authorised IFA for financial services) act as proof you've operated long enough to qualify. Membership often requires the Level 3 or 4 as a prerequisite.

**Public liability insurance** is the boring document procurement teams ask for. £1m cover starts around £80/year for low-risk service businesses; £5m is the standard ask for any contract over £10k.

**A UTR and a business bank account** at the same trading name. You can run a sole trader operation through a personal account legally, but procurement systems silently reject suppliers without a business account in 2026. Open one before the first invoice goes out.

## What the first year actually looks like, mechanically

**Month one.** Pick the offer. One sentence describing what you do and who pays. Two paying customers in the first six weeks is a better signal than a year of planning.

**Month two.** Register as a sole trader at gov.uk/log-in-register-hmrc-online-services. Open a business bank account (Tide, Mettle, Starling Business all offer free tiers with same-day approval). Set up a separate savings account labelled "Tax" and move 25-30% of every invoice into it on receipt.

**Month three.** Find a funded Level 3 course on Find a Course (gov.uk). Compare three providers. Email two with a specific question about deferral or intake timing. Enrol on the one that fits your schedule.

**Months four to six.** Build the basics. Public liability insurance if you visit client sites. A one-page website that explains what you do (Carrd, Pixpa or even a free Notion page works). Bookkeeping in a spreadsheet, not yet a £30/month subscription you don't need.

**Months seven to twelve.** Grow the customer count. Ten paying customers by month twelve is a real business. Three or fewer is a hobby with extra steps, and the honest data of that should change what you do next year.

## When the maths breaks

Stop and reset if any of these are true.

You can't find two people willing to pay actual money by month three. The offer needs reshaping, not more marketing.

Your day job is starting to suffer. The funded course is still there next intake. The mortgage doesn't pause.

Your household is paying for it in time and weekend hours. The business will wait. The relationships will not.

## What we do at Switchable

We help adults across the UK find funded training that fits the life you have now. The course finder filters business and entrepreneurship courses by your postcode, age, and prior qualifications, and surfaces providers with current places. Two minutes, no login, no long form.

{{related-posts}}

title: What 'funded training' actually means (and how to know if you qualify)
dek: 81% of eligible UK adults have never heard of fully funded training. The system was designed for them. This is what it is, who pays, and how you check eligibility in five minutes.
author: switchable_team
cover_image_alt: Adult learner reviewing course options on a tablet at a kitchen table
excerpt: UK guide to fully funded training. What 'funded' means, the four main routes (FCFJ, Skills Bootcamps, Advanced Learner Loans, apprenticeships), and how to check if you qualify.
body:
## The number that started this company

We ran a paid social campaign in 2025 asking UK adults a simple question. Had they heard of fully funded training before our ad?

81.3% said no.

These were eligible adults. People over 19, UK-resident, mostly with no Level 3 qualification. The system was built for them, on purpose, with public money. They did not know it existed.

That gap is why Switchable exists. Not to invent new training. To help adults find the routes the government has already funded.

## What "funded" actually means

"Funded" is not a discount. It is not a free trial that becomes a subscription. It is not a loan with hidden terms.

Funded training in the UK means the government pays the training provider directly. You owe nothing for the course itself. Not now, not at the end, not three years later if you get a pay rise. The money does not pass through you.

The four main routes work slightly differently. The eligibility rules differ. The shape of the course differs. The promise is the same: a regulated qualification, a recognised provider, no fee.

## The four routes worth knowing

### 1. Free Courses for Jobs (FCFJ)

A government-funded scheme that pays for a first full Level 3 qualification for adults who do not yet hold one. Level 3 is the equivalent of A-Levels or a BTEC.

Who qualifies: adults 24 and over, UK-resident, without a Level 3 already. Employed, self-employed, unemployed all count.

What is on offer: Level 3 awards, certificates and diplomas across a wide list of sectors. Business, accountancy, counselling, digital marketing, engineering, social care, hospitality. The list is set centrally and updates periodically.

How to find courses: search the government's "Find a course" service, or use a route like Switchable that filters to providers with current intakes.

### 2. Skills Bootcamps

Short, intensive training programmes funded by the Department for Education and run by approved providers, mostly in technical sectors. The format is usually twelve to sixteen weeks, part-time or full-time.

Who qualifies: adults 19 and over, UK-resident, with the right to work. Eligibility broadens for adults who have been out of work or in low-paid roles. Most bootcamps include a guaranteed interview with a hiring partner at the end.

What is on offer: software development, data, cyber, digital marketing, construction, green skills, electric vehicle maintenance. Heavy tech focus.

How to find courses: search the government's Skills Bootcamps directory by region.

### 3. Advanced Learner Loans

Not strictly free, but worth knowing about. These are government-backed loans for Level 3 to Level 6 qualifications. The loan is paid straight to the provider and you only start repaying once you earn over the threshold (currently £25,000).

Who qualifies: adults 19 and over, UK-resident. The loan is not means-tested and a credit check is not run.

What is on offer: Level 3 to Level 6 qualifications, often delivered online and self-paced.

Why it matters: if you already hold a Level 3 (so FCFJ does not apply) but want a higher qualification, the loan route gives you access without upfront fees.

### 4. Apprenticeships

Funded by the apprenticeship levy plus government top-up, apprenticeships pay for the training while the apprentice earns a wage from the employer.

Who qualifies: anyone 16 or over with no upper age limit. Adult apprenticeships are common, particularly in management, leadership, project management and digital roles.

What is on offer: every level from Level 2 right up to degree apprenticeships at Level 6 and 7. Project Management Level 4 is one of the most active adult routes.

How to find: search the government apprenticeship vacancy service, or go direct to a training provider who places candidates with employer partners.

## How to check if you qualify in five minutes

Three questions answer most cases.

Are you 19 or over? Some routes go to 24. Apprenticeships start at 16.

Are you UK-resident with the right to work or study? Settled status, pre-settled status, ILR and citizenship qualify. Visa types vary.

Do you hold a Level 3 already? A-Levels, T-Levels, BTEC Level 3, Access to HE, NVQ Level 3. If you do not, FCFJ opens up. If you do, Skills Bootcamps and Advanced Learner Loans still apply.

That is the three-question filter. There are edge cases (sector restrictions, region-specific funding, employer eligibility for apprenticeships) but the three above clear most adults.

## What it costs you (nothing, and what that does to your head)

Most people read "funded" and assume there is a catch. There is not one. The catch is psychological.

People feel that something free cannot be valuable. Or that they will be the worst person in the room. Or that the qualification will not be "real". None of these are true. The qualifications are Ofqual-regulated, the providers are inspected, and the funding exists because the country has a shortage of trained adults in sectors that need them.

The thing it actually costs is your time. A short course is forty hours. A Skills Bootcamp is several hundred. An apprenticeship is a year or more. Plan that time honestly before you enrol.

## What to do next

Take ten minutes. Run the three-question filter on yourself. Note which routes are open to you.

Then look at one provider, not ten. Pick the route that fits the life you have now. If you find a course you like and the intake date works, enrol on it. If you do not, come back next month.

{{related-posts}}
